sections.heading: When events help
sections.content: Events decouple producers from consumers and allow independent processing. A delivery-status change can feed notifications, analytics, ETA calculation and billing without the delivery service calling every consumer directly.

sections.heading: Event design
sections.content: Publish facts in past tense, such as DeliveryDispatched or StopCompleted. Include event id, aggregate id, event type, version, occurredAt and correlation id.

sections.heading: Reliability
sections.content: Use the transactional outbox pattern to persist the business change and event atomically. Consumers must be idempotent because at-least-once delivery can produce duplicates.

sections.heading: Trade-offs
sections.content: Event-driven systems improve scalability and decoupling but add eventual consistency, operational complexity and harder debugging. Use synchronous calls where an immediate response is truly required.

sections.heading: Source of truth
sections.content: Each business capability should own its authoritative data. Other services consume APIs or events instead of reading another service's tables directly.

sections.heading: Consistency choices
sections.content: Strong consistency is appropriate inside a local transaction. Across services, prefer eventual consistency with compensation, reconciliation and observable processing state.

sections.heading: Concurrency
sections.content: Use optimistic locking for competing updates to routes or deliveries. Add idempotency keys for retried commands and unique constraints as the final protection against duplicates.

sections.heading: Read models
sections.content: For dashboards and search, build read-optimized projections. A materialized view or event-fed read model avoids complex cross-domain joins on the transactional database.

sections.heading: Start with measurements
sections.content: Define expected traffic, data volume, latency targets and peak patterns. Logistics workloads often have strong morning and dispatch-time peaks.

sections.heading: Horizontal scaling
sections.content: Keep application instances stateless so they can scale horizontally. Store shared state in databases, caches or brokers and use a load balancer or Kubernetes Service.

sections.heading: Database scaling
sections.content: Use proper indexes, bounded pagination, connection-pool sizing, batching and read replicas where appropriate. Partition very large event or tracking tables by time or business key.

sections.heading: Caching
sections.content: Cache data that is expensive to calculate and safe to be slightly stale. Define TTL, invalidation and fallback behavior. Never use caching to hide an unbounded query.

sections.heading: Deployment
sections.content: Use immutable images, automated pipelines, rolling or canary deployment, health probes and a tested rollback strategy.

sections.heading: Observability
sections.content: Collect structured logs, metrics and distributed traces. Propagate correlation ids across HTTP and Kafka so one delivery flow can be followed end to end.

sections.heading: Critical metrics
sections.content: Monitor latency percentiles, error rate, throughput, queue lag, consumer failures, database pool usage, JVM memory, GC pauses and pod restarts.

sections.heading: Operational readiness
sections.content: Define SLOs, alerts, runbooks and ownership. A system is not production-ready merely because it passes functional tests.
